one_line_takeaway: Across 4,979 traces on five production LLMs, multi-agent LLM systems cut final-output privacy leakage (27.2% vs 43.2% single-agent) but open unmonitored inter-agent-message and shared-memory channels that leak at 68.8% and 46.7% respectively, so output-only audits alone miss 41.7% of actual privacy violations.
key_finding: Multi-agent coordination reduces per-channel final-output leakage (C1: 27.2% vs 43.2% single-agent, ratio 0.63x, McNemar p<0.001) but opens two new internal channels absent in single-agent systems -- inter-agent messages (C2, 68.8%, 95% CI [67.5,70.1]) and shared memory (C5, 46.7%, 95% CI [45.3,48.1]) -- that raise total system exposure (any of C1/C2/C5 leaking) to 68.9%, a 1.60x increase over the single-agent baseline. Internal channels average 57.8% leakage, 2.1x the external (C1) rate, and this C2>C1 pattern holds for every one of the five models tested (e.g., GPT-4o 76.8% vs 17.2%; Mistral Large 96.2% vs 47.5%; Claude 3.5 Sonnet 28.1% vs 3.3%) and across all four domains. 2,076 of 4,979 traces (41.7%, 95% CI [40.3,43.1]) satisfy hypothesis H1 (final output safe but C2 or C5 leaked), meaning output-only audits would miss 41.7% of actual violations; this miss rate varies sharply by domain (Healthcare 62.4%, Finance 44.9%, Legal 34.9%, Corporate 24.6%). Defenses show a stark external/internal split (A0-only defense-evaluation subset, n not the full 4,979): vanilla baseline C1=48%/internal=31%; output sanitizer cuts C1 to 1% (98% effectiveness) but leaves internal channels at 31% (0% effectiveness); privacy prompting cuts C1 60% but internal channels only 6%. All four evaluated frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGPT, LangChain, MetaGPT) show similar undefended C2 leakage (28-35%) in benign conditions, rising to the 68.8% aggregate once adversarial scenarios are included, indicating the vulnerability is architectural/systemic rather than framework-specific. Adversarial attacks are highly effective: overall ASR 79.9% (n=1,983 adversarial tests) with multi-agent coordination attacks (F4) highest at 82.9%. A representative 100-scenario secondary-channel sample found tool-input (C3) leakage 62-74% and log (C6) leakage 84-89% depending on model. Full three-tier detector performance on a 1,000-segment test set: precision 0.952, recall 0.934, F1 0.943, FPR 4.8% (vs. regex-only F1 0.148, LLM-as-Judge-alone F1 0.933); 82% of detected leaks were semantic (Tier 3) rather than pattern-matchable. A prototype sanitization interceptor (mentioned in the conclusion) reduced internal leakage from 31.5% to 2.4% at a cost of 4.7 points of task success.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AgentLeak, a benchmark for evaluating internal-channel privacy leakage in multi-agent LLM systems, built and evaluated entirely by the authors (not related work). It instruments seven privacy-relevant communication pathways (C1 final output, C2 inter-agent messages, C3 tool inputs, C4 tool outputs, C5 shared memory, C6 logs, C7 persistent artifacts) across 1,000 authored scenarios spanning healthcare, finance, legal, and corporate domains (250 each), each with a private data vault (median 29 fields, range 21-39), an allowed-disclosure set (median 3 fields, range 2-5, yielding a 9.7:1 vault-to-allowed ratio), and ground-truth labels. Scenarios are executed on five production LLMs (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Mistral Large-2411, Llama 3.3 70B-Instruct) via a coordinator-worker (2-agent) topology, adapters for four frameworks (LangChain v0.1.x, CrewAI v0.83.x, AutoGPT v0.5.x, MetaGPT v0.8.x), and a three-tier leakage detector (Tier 1 canary regex matching, Tier 2 structured/format field extraction, Tier 3 LLM-as-Judge with default judge Qwen-2.5-7B, semantic-similarity threshold tau=0.72). The large-scale empirical evaluation (n=4,979 validated paired single-agent/multi-agent traces after excluding 21 traces for timeouts/incomplete capture) focuses on channels C1, C2, and C5; C3 and C6 are evaluated on a 100-scenario representative sample; C4 and C7 are only demonstrated via an SDK/CrewAI case study, not large-scale-quantified. The paper also contributes a 32-class, 6-family (F1-F6) attack taxonomy for multi-agent coordination attacks and a three-level adversary threat model (A0 benign, A1 weak, A2 strong).
threat_model: Adversaries are graded on three capability levels. A0 (benign): no adversarial activity; leaks arise from misconfiguration, default framework behavior, or overly helpful model responses. A1 (weak adversary): manipulates external information sources (malicious web content, poisoned API responses, compromised documents) without direct system access, shaping what the system observes. A2 (strong adversary): has direct access to system components via compromised tools, malicious dependencies, or control over agent roles, covering both external attackers who compromised part of the system and malicious insiders with legitimate credentials; capabilities include prompt injection, multi-stage exfiltration chains, and exploitation of inter-agent trust. Out of scope: adversaries with administrative infrastructure access, attacks on LLM training pipelines, and UI exploits that bypass the agent architecture. The privacy definition is grounded in contextual integrity theory, operationalized as a data-minimization criterion: a leakage event occurs when a vault field outside the allowed set is detected (via semantic similarity above threshold tau) in the content of any of the seven channels.
limitations: Author-stated limitations: (1) English-only scenarios; multilingual deployments may need re-calibration and non-Latin scripts add detection challenges. (2) Tier 3 detector has a 7.4% false negative rate, so reported leakage rates are conservative lower bounds; tau=0.72 was calibrated on English data only. (3) Only four frameworks tested (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGPT, MetaGPT); newer frameworks (LangGraph, Semantic Kernel, AgentGPT) and framework updates are not covered. (4) Evaluation uses exclusively a two-agent coordinator-worker topology; hierarchical, peer-to-peer, or larger topologies were not tested. (5) LLM-as-Judge evaluation bias risk ('LLM judging LLM'), partially mitigated via deterministic Tier 1/2 anchors, held-out threshold calibration, multi-judge inter-rater agreement (kappa 0.82-0.87), and 94% human-verified accuracy on a 100-leak sample, but residual risk acknowledged. (6) The large-scale quantitative evaluation (n=4,979) covers only C1, C2, C5; C3/C6 use a 100-scenario sample and C4/C7 are only demonstrated qualitatively via an SDK case study -- the paper explicitly disclaims a complete quantitative evaluation of all seven channels. (7) Scenario coverage, while broad, cannot capture every real-world workflow; edge cases and emerging application patterns may behave differently. (8) WLS (severity-weighted leakage score) is deferred to future work since current trace capture is binary per-channel rather than per-field. (9) F5/F6 attack families (7 of 32 classes) are included in aggregate ASR but not tracked at per-trace/per-family level due to instrumentation constraints.

datasets_used: AgentLeak benchmark: 1,000 author-authored scenarios (250 each in healthcare, finance, legal, corporate), median vault 29 fields (range 21-39), median allowed set 3 fields (range 2-5)
Synthetic PII generated via the Faker library with domain-specific extensions (format-valid SSNs, Luhn-valid credit cards, hospital-convention medical record numbers)
ai4privacy/pii-masking-200k (3,624 PII samples) used to validate detection against realistic PII distributions
AGBonnet/augmented-clinical-notes (2,202 clinical notes) used to validate detection against authentic medical terminology
200-segment held-out calibration set (100 positive/100 negative) manually labeled by a domain expert, used to tune tau
1,000-segment separate test set used to report final detector precision/recall/F1/FPR
100-scenario representative sample (5 models) used for C3/C6 secondary-channel evaluation
quantitative_results: C1 (final output) leakage: 27.2% multi-agent vs 43.2% single-agent (ratio 0.63x, McNemar p<0.001)
Total system exposure (any of C1/C2/C5): 68.9% multi-agent vs 43.2% single-agent (ratio 1.60x, p<0.001)
C5 (shared memory) leakage: 46.7% (95% CI [45.3,48.1]), n=4,979; every C5 leak co-occurred with a C1 or C2 leak (incremental C5-only contribution = +0.0%)
H1 (output-only-audit-missed violations): 2,076/4,979 = 41.7% (95% CI [40.3,43.1]); by domain: Healthcare 62.4%, Finance 44.9%, Legal 34.9%, Corporate 24.6%
Per-model C2 vs C1: GPT-4o 76.8% vs 17.2%; GPT-4o-mini 75.3% vs 41.2%; Mistral Large 96.2% vs 47.5%; Llama 3.3 70B 67.8% vs 26.9%; Claude 3.5 Sonnet 28.1% vs 3.3%

Defense effectiveness (A0-only subset): Vanilla C1=48%/internal=31%; Privacy Prompt C1=19% (60% eff.)/internal=29% (6% eff.); Role Separation C1=22% (54% eff.); Output Sanitizer C1=1% (98% eff.)/internal=31% (0% eff.); LlamaGuard C1=6% (88% eff.); PromptGuard C1=8% (83% eff.)
Framework-level C2 leakage (benign, vanilla, SDK sample): CrewAI 33% [26.7,39.9]; AutoGPT 35% [27.5,43.2]; LangChain 29% [22.9,35.8]; MetaGPT 28% [19.6,37.7]

Adversarial Attack Success Rate by family (n=1,983 adversarial tests): F1 Prompt/Instruction 79.8%; F2 Tool-Surface 78.9%; F3 Memory/Persistence 79.6%; F4 Multi-Agent Coordination 82.9% (highest); Overall 79.9% [78.1,81.6]
Secondary channels (100-scenario sample, 5 models): C3 tool-input leakage 62-74%; C6 log leakage 84-89%

Detector performance on 1,000-segment test set: Regex-only F1=0.148 (precision 1.000, recall 0.080); NER+Pattern F1=0.305 (recall 0.180); LLM-as-Judge alone F1=0.933 (recall 0.926, FPR 6.2%); Full 3-tier pipeline F1=0.943 (precision 0.952, recall 0.934, FPR 4.8%)
Threshold sensitivity: tau=0.72 selected (FPR 4.8%, FNR 7.4%, F1 0.938); at tau=0.70 F1 peaks 0.942 but FPR exceeds 5%; at tau=0.75 FPR 3.5% but recall falls to 0.902
Inter-judge agreement across Qwen-2.5-7B (FPR 4.8%/FNR 7.4%), Llama-3.1-8B (5.6%/8.1%), GPT-4o-mini (4.1%/6.9%): Cohen's kappa 0.82-0.87
Human expert validation of 100 sampled detected leaks: 94% accuracy (95% CI [87.4%,97.8%])
Inter-rater reliability on allowed-set annotation: Cohen's kappa Healthcare 0.82, Finance 0.79, Legal 0.81, Corporate 0.75
4,979 valid traces from 1,000 scenarios x 5 models minus 21 exclusions (4 each GPT-4o/Claude-3.5-Sonnet/Llama-3.3-70B/Mistral-Large, 5 GPT-4o-mini)
Prototype sanitization interceptor: reduced internal leakage from 31.5% to 2.4%, at cost of 4.7-point drop in task success
Temperature sensitivity: varying temperature across [0.0,1.0] shifts leakage rates by less than 3%
baselines_compared: Single-agent (C1-only) execution vs multi-agent (coordinator-worker) execution of identical scenarios, same model, paired design (McNemar's test)
Detection ablations: Regex-only (Tier 1) vs NER/Pattern (Tiers 1+2) vs LLM-as-Judge alone (Tier 3) vs full three-tier pipeline
Defense configurations: Vanilla (no defense), Privacy Prompt, Role Separation, Output Sanitizer, PromptGuard, NeMo Guardrails, LlamaGuard 3, Lakera Guard
Four multi-agent frameworks compared for undefended C2 leakage: LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGPT, MetaGPT
Five LLMs compared: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Mistral Large-2411, Llama 3.3 70B-Instruct

AgentLeak positioned against prior benchmarks (Table 1, cited related work, not the paper's own results): AgentDojo (97 scenarios, output-only), AgentDAM (246 scenarios, web/single-agent), PrivacyLens (493 scenarios, model-level), TOP-Bench (180 scenarios, tool-only), ASB (400 scenarios, output-only)
